seoTitle: How to Copy a Table From Google Docs to Slides (2026)
metaDescription: Move a table from Google Docs into Google Slides cleanly, then resize, align, and restyle it so your data fits your presentation without clipping.
intro: Rebuilding a table from scratch in Google Slides wastes time when you've already built it in Google Docs. This guide shows how to select and copy a table structure, paste it into your presentation, and then adjust sizing, alignment, and fonts so the data fits your slide theme. It's especially useful when pulling report data into a pitch deck.

steps.heading: Select the table in Google Docs
steps.body: Click into your document and highlight the entire table or the specific rows you want to bring over, making sure the full structure is selected.

steps.heading: Copy the table data
steps.body: Use Control+C or Command+C to copy the selected cells and border data cleanly before switching applications.

steps.heading: Paste into your target slide
steps.body: Switch to Google Slides, select the slide you want the table on, and paste. You'll typically get the option to keep source formatting or paste as plain values.

steps.heading: Resize and reposition the table
steps.body: Use the bounding box handles to resize the pasted table and center it on the slide so nothing clips off the edges.

steps.heading: Match fonts and styling
steps.body: Adjust column widths, text alignment, and font colors so the imported table matches your presentation's overall theme.

faq.question: Will the pasted table stay linked to the original Google Doc?
faq.answer: No, a standard copy and paste creates a static table in Slides that won't update automatically if you change the source document.

faq.question: Why does my table look distorted after pasting into Slides?
faq.answer: This usually happens when column widths don't match the slide's available space; resizing the bounding box and adjusting individual columns typically fixes it.

faq.question: Can I paste just the table's values without the formatting?
faq.answer: Yes, most paste options let you choose to bring in unformatted values only, which is useful if you want the table to match your slide theme from scratch.

faq.question: Is there a way to keep large tables readable on a single slide?
faq.answer: Reducing font size slightly and tightening cell padding can help fit larger tables, though very large data sets may be better split across multiple slides.
